title: Chapter 5 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of Arrays & Tuples in TypeScript!

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an array in TypeScript?
options: To store a collection/list of multiple values within a single variable.
To style a web page element.
questions.explanation: Arrays group related data values into a single sequential list.

questions.question: How do you correctly type an array intended to hold exclusively strings?
questions.explanation: The standard syntax is the element type followed by empty square brackets: `string[]`.

questions.question: The syntax `let scores: Array<number>` is identical in function to what?
questions.explanation: `Array<type>` and `type[]` do exactly the same thing. `type[]` is usually preferred for its brevity.

questions.question: In TypeScript, it is generally considered a best practice to mix strings, numbers, and booleans all inside the exact same array variable.
questions.explanation: TypeScript encourages arrays to be strictly one single type to maintain robust safety and predictable iterations.

questions.question: If you initialize `let activeUsers = ["Alice", "Bob"];` without an explicit annotation, what does TypeScript do?
questions.explanation: Because all the initial items are strings, TS completely infers it as `string[]`.

questions.question: If you try to `.push(100)` into an array explicitly typed as `string[]`, what happens?
options: It crashes the web browser immediately.
TypeScript catches it and throws a compile-time error.
questions.explanation: Because the array is fully guarded as `string[]`, it outright rejects any `number` insertions.

questions.question: When you map over a `string[]` array using `.map(item => ...)`, TypeScript automatically knows `item` is a string without needing an explicit annotation.
questions.explanation: Because the array is `string[]`, TS understands every pulled item is inherently a string.

questions.question: How do you correctly type a 2-dimensional array (an array of arrays) holding strings?
questions.explanation: Stacking the brackets `[][]` indicates an array that contains inner arrays.

questions.question: A type of array that strictly enforces the exact number of elements AND their specific types in order is called a ______.
questions.explanation: Tuples lock down the exact sequence and length. For example: `[number, string]`.

questions.question: Which of these variables is a Tuple?
questions.explanation: The notation `[type, type]` creates a tuple.

questions.question: If you define a tuple `let coords: [number, number];`, you CANNOT assign it `[10, 20, 30]`.
questions.explanation: Tuples are extremely strict on length. A 2-item tuple cannot magically accept 3 items!

questions.question: In the tuple `[string, boolean]`, what happens if you try to assign `[true, "Hello"]`?
options: TypeScript throws an error because the order is backwards.
questions.explanation: Tuples enforce strict order. A `[string, boolean]` must start with a string and end with a boolean.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of Tuples over standard Arrays?
options: They are faster to loop through.
They provide absolute certainty about the type of data at every specific index.
questions.explanation: For a tuple `[string, number]`, TS knows with 100% certainty that index [0] is a string and index [1] is a number.

questions.question: If you create an empty array explicitly like `let prices: number[] = [];`, is this valid?
options: Yes, you are safely preparing an empty array that will only accept numbers later.
No, you must provide at least one number.
No, TS will convert it to `any[]`.
No, an empty array cannot be typed.
questions.explanation: This is completely valid and highly recommended! It sets up an empty container that enforces type safety globally.

questions.question: You can never create an array of booleans `boolean[]` in TypeScript; arrays are only for strings and numbers.
questions.explanation: You can create an array of literally any type in TypeScript, including booleans!

questions.question: What common data format heavily utilizes Tuple-like structures where each index means a very specific thing (e.g., [ID, Name, Email])?
questions.explanation: CSV rows perfectly mirror tuple structures because every row has exactly the same column types.

questions.question: If you don't explicitly type an empty array `let list = [];`, what does TypeScript cautiously assume its type is?
questions.explanation: Without knowing what you want to put in it later, TS has no choice but to default it to the extremely unsafe `any[]`.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly initiates an array of strings using the `Array<T>` generic notation?
questions.explanation: The `<string>` notation informs the base `Array` interface what type it is holding.

questions.question: You can push a `number` into a tuple typed as `[number, string]` if you are pushing it to the end of the tuple via `.push()`.
questions.explanation: Interestingly, due to an older quirk in TS, `.push()` is not fully restricted by tuple length limits, but direct assignment `[1, 'a', 2]` is!

questions.question: If you assign `let colors = ["red", "blue"];`, and later try `colors[0] = 5;`, what occurs?
options: TypeScript throws an error because it inferred `colors` as `string[]`.
The first index becomes a number and the rest remain strings.
questions.explanation: TS inferred that the array only takes strings, so attempting to assign a number to an index throws a Type Error.
